principle: Each user arrives at your site with a reservoir of goodwill that depletes with every friction point — hiding phone numbers, punishing input formats and asking unnecessary data drain it faster than any visual design can refill it
explanation: X.ux|goodwill_reservoir: mental_model: each_user_enters_with_reservoir_of_patience; depletes: hiding_support_phone -> user_angrier_when_they_find_it + requiring_dash_format_in_CC_field + asking_data_not_needed_for_task + marketing_bloat_before_content + sloppy_appearance; special_case: single_mistake_can_empty_instantly(registration_wall_before_purchase); reservoir_varies: impatient_users_start_low + bad_prior_session_lowers_starting_level; refills_via: upfront_prices+shipping_cost + making_top_3_tasks_dead_obvious + saving_steps(auto-submit_tracking_link) + real_FAQs_not_marketing_pitches; NO:hiding_costs_until_last_step NO:requiring_specific_format_when_stripping_it_costs_one_line_of_code NO:signup_wall_before_any_value; eg: airport_wifi_3_pages_before_price_reveal >> price_visible_on_connection_page

principle: Mobile First as a content strategy failed because it assumed users on mobile devices are always on the move — in reality people use mobile everywhere including from the couch and expect every feature
explanation: X.ux|mobile_context_myth: mobile_first_theory: design_minimal_set_for_on_the_move_user; reality: users_equally_likely_mobile_on_couch_at_home; result: stripped_mobile_sites_frustrated_users_who_want_full_feature_set; correct_mobile_first: forces_identifying_TRULY_essential_content(always_good) NOT strip_by_assumed_context; principle: if_someone_wants_feature_and_on_mobile -> they_still_want_that_feature; real_mobile_tradeoffs: prioritize_frequent+urgent_tasks_to_home_screen + deeper_nav_acceptable_for_rare_features; managing_real_estate_challenges_must_NOT_cost_usability; NO:removing_features_because_nobody_uses_them_on_mobile NO:separate_stripped_mobile_site; eg: bank_app_no_account_opening_on_mobile >> full_feature_parity_with_common_tasks_at_surface

principle: Apps must be memorable between sessions not just learnable in the first session — users who must re-learn how to operate an app every time they return will abandon it because life is cheap at 99 cents
explanation: X.ux|app_memorability: learnability = can_user_figure_it_out_first_time; memorability = can_user_remember_how_next_time_without_re-learning; both_required: learnable_only -> every_session_feels_like_first -> frustration -> abandonment; pattern: innovative_novel_gestures + no_persistent_help -> user_forgets_between_uses -> relearns_frustratedly; low_switching_cost(0.99$) == high_abandonment_risk; fix: (1)familiar_gesture_conventions_where_possible (2)controls_visible_or_consistently_discoverable (3)help_findable_anytime_not_only_at_launch; NO:tutorial_only_at_first_launch_never_accessible_again NO:novel_interaction_for_every_core_action; eg: drawing_app_controls_invisible_on_screen -> forget_each_session >> controls_accessible_via_consistent_gesture_OR_always_visible_icon

principle: When testing reveals users miss something the instinct is to add explanation — but the right fix is usually to remove what is obscuring it, and self-correcting errors require no fix at all
explanation: X.ux|debrief_remove_not_add: testing_reveals_confusion -> team_reflex: add_explanation+instruction+tooltip; reality: confusion_caused_by_noise_obscuring_signal NOT missing_signal; right_fix: remove_obscuring_element -> signal_becomes_visible; adding_more: compounds_clutter -> harder_to_find; kayak_rule: user_goes_astray_but_self-corrects_quickly(all_three: notices+recovers+unperturbed) -> NO_fix_needed; feature_requests: treat_skeptically -> ask_participant_to_describe_full_working_version -> they_usually_conclude_they_wouldn_t_use_it; focus_ruthlessly_on_fixing_most_serious_problems_first; NO:adding_popup_tip_when_user_missed_button NO:acting_on_wish-list_without_full_description_test; eg: users_miss_checkout_CTA -> add_text_label >> users_miss_CTA_because_hero_steals_attention -> remove_hero -> CTA_visible

principle: Screen reader users scan with their ears the same way sighted users scan with their eyes — they listen to the first few words of each link and skip if irrelevant, making front-loaded link text the single most important accessibility fix
explanation: X.ux|screen_reader_scan: sighted_user: eye_jumps_to_bold+headings+links; screen_reader_user: listens_at_high_speed(2x+) + skips_to_next_link_after_first_few_words; consequence: keyword_at_END_of_link -> never_heard; fatal_pattern: "Click_here_to_download_the_report" -> user_hears_"Click_here" -> skips; fix: front-load_meaningful_word: "Download_accessibility_report" NOT "Click_here_to_access_our_report"; applies_to: link_text + first_words_of_paragraphs + heading_labels; screen_reader_users_as_impatient_as_sighted_users -> long_verbose_pages_hurt_both; NO:"learn_more" NO:"click_here" NO:meaningful_word_buried_at_end_of_link; eg: "Click_here_for_pricing" >> "Pricing_details"
